coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent hydrogel were developed for Fe(II) detection to overcome the shortcomings of simple carbon dots solutions (CDs), which are difficult to store and use. CDs were prepared using the hydrothermal synthesis with o-phenylenediamine and dopamine as the carbon sources. Fluorescent hydrogel (CACDs) were obtained by doping CDs in and impregnating them with carboxymethyl cellulose hydrogel (CA) prepared using crosslinked carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC), which was grafted using acrylamide as the substrate. Using CDs and CA as a fluorescent group and a backbone, respectively, enhanced the fluorescence performance and facilitated Fe(II) detection. The surface groups, elemental composition and optical properties of the prepared CDs and CACDs were systematically studied using FTIR, XRD, UV–Vis and fluorescence spectrometer. The effects of pH, temperature, NaCl salt solution and UV irradiation on the stability of CDs in the hydrogel were discussed. CACDs exhibited strong, bright yellow fluorescence with a fluorescence intensity 4.35 times higher than that of CDs. CACDs can be used as fluorescent probes to realise real-time detection of Fe(II) at a low detection limit (0.22 μM), allowing them to be employed for Fe(II) detection in real water samples. CACDs had stable adsorption properties for Fe(II). The adsorption process of metal ions conformed to pseudo-second order kinetics and Langmuir model, respectively. Therefore, CACDs are expected to be bifunctional materials for effective detection and removal of Fe(II).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram and mechanism of the fluorescent hydrogel preparation process.
Characterization of microstructures of CDs and CACDs. (a) XRD plots of CDs; (b) SEM of CA; (c) SEM of CACDs; (d) FTIR spectra of CDs, CA and CACDs.

Fluorescence properties of CDs. (a) fluorescence emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths; (b) Plot of normalized UV–Vis absorption spectra, fluorescence excitation spectra, and fluorescence emission spectra, insets are optical photographs in natural light (left) and under UV light (right).

Fluorescence properties of CACDs. (a)Fluorescence emission spectra (λex = 420 nm) of CACDs spiked with different concentrations of CDs (0.50–2.25 mg/mL); (b) 3D fluorescence profiles; (c) Fluorescence emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths; (d) Normalized UV–Vis absorption spectra, fluorescence excitation spectra and fluorescence emission spectra maps, insets are optical photographs in natural light (left) and under UV light (right).

Fluorescence stability test of CACDs. (a) Fluorescence emission spectra in the range of pH 2–12 (λex = 420 nm), the inset shows the corresponding histogram of fluorescence ratios; (b) Fluorescence emission spectra in the range of temperatures 40–75 °C (λex = 420 nm); (c) Folded line graphs of fluorescence intensity ratios in NaCl solutions with concentrations of 0–0.5 M; (d) Fluorescence intensities at different times of irradiation by UV light at 365 nm ratio line graph.

Ion detection performance of CACDs. (a) Fluorescence emission spectra after addition of various metal ions to CDs (λex = 420 nm); (b) Fluorescence emission spectra after addition of various metal ions to CACDs (λex = 420 nm); (c) Normalized UV–Vis absorption spectra of CACDs and normalized fluorescence excitation spectra and fluorescence emission spectra of CDs; (d) Fluorescence response of CACDs to various metal ions and subsequent addition of Fe(II) Fluorescence response histograms of the CACDs.
Optical photographs under natural light (top) and UV light (bottom) after addition of various metal ions to CACDs.

Selective sensitivity of CACDs to Fe(II). (a) Fluorescence emission spectra (λex = 420 nm) of CACDs after the addition of different concentrations of Fe(II) (0–1000 μM) to the CACDs; (b) Plot of fluorescence intensity ratios (F/F0) of the CACDs versus Fe(II) concentration.
Schematic of the detection mechanism of CACDs for Fe(II).

Adsorption measurement. (a)The equilibrium adsorption amount of CA and CACDs to Fe(II); (b) pseudo-first order and (c) pseudo-second order kinetics plots for the adsorption of the Fe(II) on CA and CACDs; (d) Relationship between adsorption of CA and CACDs and Fe(II) concentration; (e) Langmuir model and (f) Freundlich model for CACDs on Fe(II).
Recent reports on CDs and CDs hydrogels for ferrous detection and their properties.
Detection of Fe(II) in real water samples using the CACDs.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots-based hydrogel fluorescent composites for Fe(II) detection and separation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent hydrogel were developed for Fe(II) detection to overcome the shortcomings of simple carbon dots solutions (CDs), which are difficult to store and use. CDs were prepared using the hydrothermal synthesis with o-phenylenediamine and dopamine as the carbon sources. Fluorescent hydrogel (CACDs) were obtained by doping CDs in and impregnating them with carboxymethyl cellulose hydrogel (CA) prepared using crosslinked carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC), which was grafted using acrylamide as the substrate. Using CDs and CA as a fluorescent group and a backbone, respectively, enhanced the fluorescence performance and facilitated Fe(II) detection. The surface groups, elemental composition and optical properties of the prepared CDs and CACDs were systematically studied using FTIR, XRD, UV–Vis and fluorescence spectrometer. The effects of pH, temperature, NaCl salt solution and UV irradiation on the stability of CDs in the hydrogel were discussed. CACDs exhibited strong, bright yellow fluorescence with a fluorescence intensity 4.35 times higher than that of CDs. CACDs can be used as fluorescent probes to realise real-time detection of Fe(II) at a low detection limit (0.22 μM), allowing them to be employed for Fe(II) detection in real water samples. CACDs had stable adsorption properties for Fe(II). The adsorption process of metal ions conformed to pseudo-second order kinetics and Langmuir model, respectively. Therefore, CACDs are expected to be bifunctional materials for effective detection and removal of Fe(II).

We have produced a novel yellow fluorescent emitting carbon dots, which were doped carbon dots in and impregnated them with carboxymethyl cellulose hydrogel to obtain a carbon dots-based fluorescent hydrogel. The fluorescent hydrogel was hydrophilic, biocompatible and stable, with a fluorescence intensity 4.35 times higher than that of carbon dots. Importantly, the carbon dots-based fluorescent hydrogel allows specific detection of Fe(II) in real water samples with a detection limit of 0.22 μM.
Unlabelled Image

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Iron (Fe) is the most abundant redox-active metallic element in living organisms, and it is widely distributed in nature, mainly in soil, numerous minerals, rocks, seawater, groundwater and industrial wastes [1,2].Dissolved Fe is primarily present in water in two redox states: ferrous (Fe(II)) ions and ferric (Fe(III)) ions. Bioavailable Fe (mainly Fe(II)) is considered a limiting factor for marine primary producers and phytoplankton growth because its oxidised state and ligand complexation affect the Fe content in water [3,4]. Fe ions are essential in several physiological and pathological activities. In fact, several serious diseases, including cancer [5], neurodegenerative diseases [6], hepatitis [7] and mesothelioma [8], are associated with oxidative stress and excessive accumulation of Fe in the body. In addition, corrosion of steel in distribution pipes may lead to iron contamination in drinking water, and the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) has set a secondary standard of 0.3 mg/L for iron in drinking water [9].

Accurate separation and measurement of Fe(II) and Fe(III) is challenging; however, selective detection of more water-soluble Fe(II) is necessary for redox processes [10]. Current assays focus on the detection of Fe(III) and Fe [11–13]. Thus, developing a fast and accurate method for Fe(II) detection is crucial. At present, atomic absorption spectrometry [14], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [15], Spectrophotometry [16], X-ray fluorescence [17] and radioisotope [18] methods can be used for quantitative and qualitative analyses of Fe(II). However, these methods require pre-processing, complex instrumentation and lengthy processes, which limit their practical applications. Thus, we propose a simple, rapid, inexpensive and highly sensitive fluorescence method for the detection of Fe(II), which allows for the selective detection and separation of metal ions [19,20].

Carbon dots (CDs) are a new type of carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials. CDs typically comprise amorphous shells containing numerous surface functional groups and sp2 hybridised-nucleated carbon nanocrystals. In recent years, they have been widely used in heavy-metal-ion detection [21,22], catalysis [23], biosensing [24], drug delivery and bioimaging [25,26] owing to their green synthesis, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, excellent fluorescence properties and photostability. Based on the carbon source, the preparation methods of CDs can be categorised into ‘bottom-up’ and ‘top-down’. Among the ‘bottom-up’ methods, hydrothermal synthesis is preferred for its simplicity, environmental friendliness and sustainability. Kolekar et al. [27] prepared blue-emitting CDs via hydrothermal synthesis using crude sugar as a carbon precursor, and the CDs exhibited selectivity towards Cr(VI) and Fe(III). Ma et al. [28] synthesised blue-fluorescent CDs via a hydrothermal method using ammonium citrate and DL-methionine as precursors for highly accurate and sensitive recognition of Hg(II) and ampicillin. However, most blue-emitting CDs cause damage to cells and organisms [29]. Thus, developing long-wavelength CDs is crucial for the use of CDs in detection. Therefore, researchers have developed more CDs with long-wavelength emission, including green-, yellow- and red-emitting CDs. Tall et al. [30] obtained N, P-doped green-emitting CDs via a hydrothermal synthesis using phytic acid and L-arginine as a raw material for determination of Cr(VI) in water and soil samples. Dong et al. [31] obtained orange-emitting CDs using p-phenylenediamine as a raw material and ethambutol as a solvent for the construction of a biosensing platform to detect pH in living cells. CDs can be used as probes for direct and rapid on-site detection of metal ions in environmental wastewater [32]. However, the presence of organic substances in actual wastewater induces the aggregation of hydrophilic CDs, leading to self-absorption of their fluorescence and the reduction of its intensity [33]. Most hydrophilic CDs are soluble in water and difficult to remove from it, resulting in secondary contamination. To overcome these problems, suitable carriers are necessary for dispersing and immobilising CDs [34].

Hydrogel are functional carriers [35] with 3D polymer networks formed through physical or chemical crosslinking, which provides several binding sites for small molecules. Combining CDs and hydrogel enhances their individual properties. Compared with CDs, CDs-based hydrogel prevent fluorescence quenching caused by CDs aggregation [36] and enhance their fluorescence stability. Compared with hydrogel, CDs-based hydrogel exhibit enhanced mechanical [37], self-healing [38] and adsorption [39] properties as well as new fluorescence [40,41] properties. Therefore, multifunctional CDs-based hydrogel formed via combining CDs and hydrogel have broader application prospects. Fluorescent hydrogel exhibit high sensitivity and fast response in the detection of metal ions owing to their hydrophilic and porous three-dimensional (3D) polymer network structure. Zhou and his team [42] used yellow fluorescent emitting nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCDs) prepared from salicylic acid and o-phenylenediamine and composited with oxidised arabinogel-gelatin-based hydrogel (NAH) to obtain a fluorescent sensor capable of selectively detecting Cr(VI). The linear range was 0–90 μM, and the detection limit was 0.19 μM. The maximum theoretical adsorption capacity of the NAH hydrogel was 169.4 mg/g. Wen et al. [43] grafted CDs prepared from chitosan as a carbon source onto a sodium alginate-gelatin hydrogel membrane to form a fluorescent hydrogel film (FHGF). The tensile stress of the fluorescent film was significantly increased after CDs doping. The fluorescent film was well quenched by Fe(III) in the concentration range of 0–1.8 μM, and the detection limit was as low as 0.043 μM. In recent years, polysaccharides have been widely used to prepare hydrogel because they are biocompatible, abundant and inexpensive. Carboxymethylcellulose (CMC) [44,45] is a natural polymer with unique biodegradability, biocompatibility and film-forming properties. It is derived from cellulose through the introduction of carboxymethyl (-CH2COOH) [46].CDs can be uniformly dispersed in the CMC structure through electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonding mechanisms owing to the numerous -OH and -COOH functional groups in the CMC structure [47,48]. Thus, carboxymethyl cellulose hydrogels with stable fluorescence properties can be prepared. Combining CDs and hydrogel enhances their individual properties. To the best of our knowledge, there are limited studies on detecting Fe(II) ions using fluorescent hydrogel. Thus, developing fluorescent hydrogel capable of detecting isolated Fe(II) ions with carboxymethyl cellulose hydrogel (CA) as scaffolds and CDs as fluorescence-based units is extremely important in the field of fluorescent sensors for metal detection.

Herein, o-phenylenediamine and dopamine were used as carbon sources to prepare CDs, which were doped into CA to obtain CDs-based fluorescent hydrogel. First, we prepared CDs with yellow fluorescence under a 365 nm UV light using a hydrothermal synthesis. CA were prepared by thermally initiated free radical polymerisation using crosslinked CMC grafted with acrylamide as the substrate as well as potassium persulfate (KPS) and N, N´-methylene-bis-(acrylamide) (MBA) as the initiator and crosslinker, respectively. CDs were impregnated with and doped into the CA to obtain CDs-based fluorescent hydrogel (CACDs). CDs endowed the hydrogel with excellent fluorescence properties, and the hydrogen bonds formed between the hydrogel and CDs improved the stability of CDs. This synthesis method is simple, inexpensive and safe without pollution, and the prepared CACDs exhibit good fluorescence properties and stability with a low detection limit (0.22 μM) for Fe(II). The results indicate the potential application of CACDs as an effective portable platform for visual detection and stable adsorption of hazardous metal ions.

Dopamine (C8H11NO2, 98.00 %), potassium persulfate (KPS, 99.99 %) and acrylamide (AM, 99.00 %) purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (China). O-phenylenediamine (C6H8N2, 98.00 %) was purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute (China). Sodium carboxymethyl cellulose (Na-CMC, sodium content (6.50–8.50 %)) was purchased from Tianjin Fuchen Chemical Reagent Factory (China). N, N′-methylene bisacrylamide (MBA, average Mw 154.17) was purchased from Shanghai Chemical Reagent Company of China Pharmaceutical Group (China). Sodium hydroxide (NaOH, average Mw 40.00), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37 %), and sodium chloride (NaCl, average Mw 58.44) were purchased from Beijing Chemical Factory (China). All these chemicals were of analytical grade and were used without further purification. All experiments used ultrapure water obtained using a Milli-Q purification system (Merck, Germany).

A SmartLab X-ray diffractometer (XRD) (Rigaku, Japan) was used to study the internal structure of CDs. Elements in CDs were analysed using a JSM-5610LV X-ray energy spectrometer (Electronics, Japan). Ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were measured using a Cary 5000 ultraviolet-visible-near infrared (UV–Vis-NIR) spectrometer (Agilent, USA). A Nicolet IS50 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR) (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) was used to analyze the surface functional groups. An LS-55 fluorescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer, USA) was used for photoluminescence spectrum (PL) analysis. In addition, CA and CACDs were tested in cyclic compression on a universal testing machine (Shimadzu, Japan). Cylindrical samples (20 mm height, 15 mm diameter) were prepared for compression testing of hydrogel.

1 mmol of o-phenylenediamine and 0.5 mmol of dopamine were dissolved ultrasonically in 20 mL of ultrapure water, and then the mixture was placed in a PTFE-lined reactor (50 mL) at 200 °C for 5 h. After the reaction, it was cooled naturally to room temperature, and centrifugation at 10,000 rpm was performed to remove large agglomerated particles to obtain a brownish-red solution. The solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane to remove aggregated particles and dialyzed using dialysis tubing with a molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 100 Da for 24 h. During the dialysis process, the ultrapure water was changed every 3 h. The solution was purified and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C. The purified CDs solution was partially stored in a 4 °C refrigerator for backup. Solid CDs powder was obtained after vacuum drying.

Novel hydrogel were synthesised using the thermally initiated free radical polymerisation method. A 2 wt% solution of carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) was configured, and 5 g of acrylamide (AM), 0.04 g of N, N′-methylene bisacrylamide (MBA), and 0.06 g of potassium persulfate (KPS) were added to 20 g of CMC solution, and the solid was stirred until it was completely dissolved at room temperature, and then homogeneously mixed and polymerized at 45 °C for 5 h. The prepared hydrogel was rinsed and soaked in ultrapure water to eliminate residual reagents to obtain the hydrogel. The prepared hydrogel was rinsed and soaked in ultrapure water to eliminate the residual reagents to obtain the hydrogel. The CDs were configured into different concentrations and dispersed homogeneously using ultrasonication, and the obtained hydrogel were placed in an oven at 25 °C for 10 h. The hydrogel were soaked in different concentrations of (0.50 mg/mL, 0.75 mg/mL, 1.00 mg/mL, 1.25 mg/mL, 1.50 mg/mL, 1.75 mg/mL, 2.00 mg/ mL, 2.25 mg/mL) CDs solution was immersed for 4 h to obtain CDs-doped hydrogels. The optimal doping ratio of CDs was named CACDs. At the same time, CDs-free hydrogel were prepared by the same method, which was named CA.

The Fe (II) selectivity of CACDs relative to other metal ions was studied using the following metal ions: Fe(II), K(I), Ag(I), Na(I), Cr(III), Cu(II), Mn(II), Fe(III), Mg(II), Cr(VI), Al(III), Zn(II), Co(II), Ca(II), Hg(II) and Ni(II). Standard ion solutions were prepared at a concentration of 0.1 M to study the change in fluorescence intensity of the hydrogel. The hydrogel were incubated in different metal ion solutions for 5 min and the changes in their fluorescence curves were recorded at 420 nm excitation wavelength. The visually produced colour changes were observed under 365 nm UV light and natural light, respectively, and photographs were taken and stored.

Different concentrations of Fe(II) solutions (0–1000 μM) were pre-prepared to detect the sensitivity of CACDs to Fe(II). CACDs were immersed in different concentrations of Fe(II) solutions and incubated at room temperature for 5 min to measure the fluorescence intensity. The visually produced colour changes were observed under 365 nm UV light and natural light, respectively, and photographs were taken and stored.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Standard addition detection of Fe(II) in actual water samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to verify the application of CACDs in real water samples, the spiked recovery method was used to determine South Lake in Changchun, tap water and bottled mineral water. Tap water and mineral water were obtained from the laboratory and local supermarkets, respectively, and could be used directly without any pretreatment. The lake water samples were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min, and the supernatant was collected and filtered through a 0.22 μM filter membrane. The CACDs were immersed in actual water samples with different concentrations (15 μM, 30 μM, and 50 μM) of Fe(II), and the fluorescence intensity was measured, and the recoveries were calculated and averaged three times, respectively.

Fig. 1 illustrates the preparation of fluorescent hydrogel. CDs with uniform size and high fluorescence quantum yield were prepared by one-pot hydrothermal method (200 °C, 5 h) using o-phenylenediamine (1 mmol) and dopamine (0.5 mmol) as carbon sources. CA were prepared by grafting AM onto CMC backbone via a free radical grafting polymerisation method using KPS and MBA as an initiator and a crosslinker, respectively. During the preparation process, KPS was thermally decomposed into sulphate anionic radicals, which extracted hydrogen from the -OH and -COOH and other groups of CMC. These alkoxyl groups on the CMC backbone acted as active centres capable of triggering the radical polymerisation of AM and crosslinking of MBA to form a composite hydrogel with a 3D network structure [49–51]. The -NH2 and -OH on the surface of CDs easily combine with H(I) to form hydrogen bonds. Moreover, the molecular chain of CMC contains numerous -OH and -COOH, which combine to form a densely crosslinked hydrogel with the dual functions of CDs and hydrogel. Owing to the electrostatic interactions between CDs and the CA, CDs can be stably dispersed in CA [52]. The CDs encapsulated in the 3D network structure endow the hydrogel with new fluorescence properties, and the hydrogel improves the stability of the CDs through electrostatic interactions [53].

A series of X-ray diffraction (XRD), energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS), scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was conducted to investigate the structures of CDs and CACDs. XRD analysis was conducted to determine the crystalline and amorphous nature of CDs (Fig. 2a). The broadened peak at 20.1 indicates that the synthesised CDs exhibit typical amorphous carbon structures [54]. To determine the elemental composition of the CDs, they were analysed using EDS (Fig. S1a). The results indicate that C, N and O were uniformly distributed in the CDs. SEM was used to determine the morphology of the CA (Fig. 2b), which exhibit 3D porous network structures. These 3D network structures can increase the ion migration rate and enhance the adsorption capacity of the material [55]. Fig. 2c shows the SEM of CACDs, the structure of which is denser than that of the CA with a smaller pore size. This is mainly due to the hydrogen bonding between the CDs and the molecular chains of the hydrogel, suggesting that CDs incorporation in the hydrogel changes its internal structure. The chemical structures of CACDs, the CA and CDs were analysed using FTIR. As shown in Fig. 2d, CACDs retained most of the characteristic peaks of CDs and CA; however, all peaks were slightly reduced compared to those of the original materials. The peaks observed at 3415 and 2914 cm−1 were attributed to the O-H/N-H and CH stretching vibrations, respectively [56–59]. In the CACDs spectra, the peak intensity of the O-H/N-H groups was higher than that of the CA, probably owing to the formation of hydrogen bonds between CDs and the hydrogel [49,60]. The disappearance of the characteristic peaks of CDs (of CC and CC) at 1611 and 1496 cm−1, respectively, suggests that the CDs may be reacting with the hydrogel networks. In addition, compared to the CA spectra, a new peak attributed to CC occurred at 1608 cm−1 in the CACDs spectra [61]. Moreover, the intensity of the CO peak increased and the peak shifted from 1651 to 1661 cm−1 after graft polymerisation, indicating the successful grafting of CDs on the CA [50]. The FTIR spectra of the CACDs were almost the same as those of the CA, confirming the physical interaction between the CDs and the polymer chains.

The effect of different CMC contents on the mechanical properties of hydrogels was investigated. The analysis was carried out by tensile tests (Fig. S2a) to assess the elastic modulus and toughness (Fig. S2b). As shown in Fig. S2a, the fracture stress values of the hydrogels were 65, 165, 253, and 181 kPa, and the strain values were 1165, 1876, 3146, and 2448 % when the CMC content was 1.0 wt%, 1.5 wt%, 2.0 wt%, and 2.5 wt%, respectively. With the increase of CMC content, the hydrogel fracture stress and strain showed a significant increase. Notably, as shown in Fig. S2b, the fracture toughness reached 2693 kJ/m3 at a CMC content of 2.0 wt%, indicating a further increase in the toughness of the hydrogel. The enhanced mechanical strength of the hydrogels is mainly due to the presence of many non-covalent bonds that lead to chain entanglement. The cyclic compression characteristics of the CACDs were tested using a universal testing machine, which applied pressure to the hydrogel (Figs. S3). Under compression that changed the height of the hydrogel from 2.5 to 0.25 cm (90 % deformation), the compressive strength of the CA increased to 474 kPa and that of the CACDs increased to a certain extent (570 kPa). After removing the pressure, both hydrogel fully recovered, suggesting that CDs incorporation can enhance the compressive properties of the hydrogel. CACDs were compressed ten times, showing a strain of 90 % and exhibiting tiny hysteresis return lines, which indicate their excellent compression recovery.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence properties of CDs and hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: CDs have good optical properties. Aqueous solutions of CDs are yellow under natural light. Herein, a strong yellow fluorescence was observed when the CDs were exposed to UV light (365 nm, inset of Fig. 3b). The fluorescence properties of the CDs were further investigated using fluorescence spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 3a, the CDs exhibited single-emission fluorescence characteristics, and the maximum emission intensity was obtained at 564 nm and 25.38 % quantum yield when the excitation wavelength was 420 nm, indicating the formation of yellow fluorescent CDs. This is in good agreement with the fact that CDs emit yellow emission in solutions under UV light (365 nm). The emission wavelength of CDs did not change within a wide excitation range (390–450 nm), indicating the stable fluorescence of CDs in solutions. The optical absorption properties of the hydrogel were investigated by obtaining the UV–Vis absorption spectra of the CDs (Fig. 3b). The absorption peak at 283 nm, which corresponds to the π-π* jump of CC, indicated that the CDs strongly absorbed UV light. The absorption peak at 415 nm is unique to nitrogen-doped CDs. Owing to the presence of NH2 on the surface of nitrogen-doped CDs, the conjugation of the whole system increased the red shift of the π-π* CN bond to 415 nm [62,63].

CACDs were synthesised through the reaction of -NH2 on the surface of CDs with the -OH and -COOH in the CA to form hydrogen bonds (Fig. 1). A series of hydrogels with different CDs concentrations was prepared through adjusting the ratio of CDs to the CA (Fig. 4a). CACDs at different CDs concentrations exhibited different colors. With the increase in the CDs concentration, the yellow colour of CACDs gradually deepened under daylight became a dark bright yellow under UV light (Fig. S4). Therefore, the colour change of CACDs may be attributed to the CDs concentration. By varying the CDs concentration, the optimal emission wavelength of the obtained fluorescent hydrogels was determined to be 565–586 nm, and 1.00 mg/mL CDs doping ratio hydrogel exhibited the optimal fluorescence intensity. Thus, the optimal doping ratio selected for further analysis in this experiment was that of 1.00 mg/mL CDs doping ratio hydrogel. By comparing the effects of different CMC contents and cross-linking (N, N′-methylene bisacrylamide (MBA), divinylbenzene (DVB), diisocyanate (MDI), epichlorohydrin (ECH)) agents on the fluorescence intensity of the hydrogels (Fig. S5), the optimal CMC content of 2.0 wt% and the optimal cross-linking agent MBA were selected. As shown in the inset of Fig. 4d, the CACDs recorded under 365 nm UV excitation light exhibited yellow fluorescence, indicating that the CDs can show fluorescence behaviour even when doped into CA. To further explore the fluorescence behaviour of CACDs, detailed fluorescence characterization was performed at different excitation wavelengths (390–450 nm). As shown in Fig. 4c, the highest fluorescence intensity can be observed in the emission spectrum of CACDs under excitation (420 nm), which is in good agreement with the emission peak of CDs (Fig. 3a). This implies that the CDs were mainly connected to the CMC and AM polymer molecules through their surface groups rather than through the carbon core of the CDs. Thus, the number and type of surface defects (surface oxygen and/or nitrogen functional groups) of CDs remained essentially the same in the CACDs, and there was no noticeable change in the surface energy traps. Therefore, CACDs and CDs exhibited the same fluorescence properties. The emission wavelength of CACDs did not change within a wide excitation range (390–450 nm), indicating the stable fluorescence of CACDs. The fluorescence intensity of CACDs under excitation (420 nm) was 4.35 times higher than that of CDs, and CACDs exhibited higher fluorescence performance (Fig. S7a). This can be attributed to the dense, pore-like structure within the hydrogel that allows a uniform distribution of CDs in them and enhances the stability of CDs [64]. Therefore, CACDs can improve fluorescence sensitivity [65,66]. The optical absorption properties of CACDs were investigated using UV–Vis absorption spectra (Fig. 4d). CACDs exhibited absorption peaks at 283 and 443 nm, which indicate that CDs absorb light within a wide wavelength range (250–550 nm). The absorption peak at 284 nm corresponded to that of the CDs solution at 283 nm, which can be attributed to the π-π* jump of CC, confirming the successful doping of CDs into the CA in terms of photoresponse. This is in good agreement with the results of the CC analysis of the FTIR (Fig. 2d). The absorption peak at 443 nm corresponded to that of the CDs solution at 415 nm [51], which corresponded to the π-π* leaps in the sp2 structural domain of aromatic, confirming the successful doping of CDs into the CA and the photo responsiveness of CACDs.

The effects of pH, temperature, NaCl salt solution and UV irradiation on the fluorescence stability of CDs in the CA were investigated. CDs-based hydrogel can change their morphology and function in response to external stimuli such as pH, temperature [67]. The fluorescence intensity of CACDs was measured at an excitation wavelength of 420 nm and a pH range of 2.0–12.0 to investigate its change with the pH (Fig. 5a). At pH < 6.0, a low fluorescence intensity was observed because of the non-radiative complex centres formed on the surface of the CACDs owing to the protonation of the surface functional groups. As the pH increased to 9.0, CACDs exhibited a stable and strong fluorescence intensity. A higher fluorescence intensity of CACDs was observed at pH > 9.0, showing the highest fluorescence intensity at pH = 11.0, mainly because the change in the solution alkalinity leads to the deprotonation of CACDs in the hydrogel, which results in changes in the surface structure and state of the CACDs, consequently causing changes in the fluorescence properties [62,63,68]. In addition, the fluorescence emission peaks of CDs red shifted in acidic solutions. This is probably because in this low-pH environment, CDs are present in the form of isolated CDs in the fluorescent hydrogel. With the increase in pH, the interaction among non-covalent bonds, such as hydrogen bonding among -OH, -COOH and -NH2 groups, was enhanced, leading to the aggregation of CDs [69]. The fluorescence intensities of the CACDs did not exhibit a substantial linear pH-dependent pattern, and hence, we did not investigate the effect of pH on the fluorescence intensities in depth in this experiment. The highest fluorescence intensity was observed at pH = 7.0. Therefore, pH = 7.0 was set as the ideal pH for fluorescence detection. Temperature exhibited a considerable effect on the fluorescence properties of the prepared CACDs. The temperature of CACDs was controlled at 40°-75°, and the change in the fluorescence intensity with temperature was investigated via the water bath method (λex = 420 nm). As shown in Fig. 5b, the fluorescence of the hydrogel decreased with increasing the temperature. This may be due to thermally activated non-radiative trapping [70], where the non-radiative channel opens with increasing temperature and the non-radiative lifetime decreases with increasing temperature, leading to a decrease in quantum yield and fluorescence intensity. Salt tolerance testing was conducted by adding CACDs to a NaCl salt solution (0–0.5 M) and leaving it to stand for 5 min (Fig. 5c). The fluorescence intensity of the CACDs at the optimal excitation wavelength was stable within the salt-solution-concentration test range, indicating that CACDs can be used for detecting low ionic strengths. The CACDs were irradiated under a 365 nm UV light for 60 min, and the fluorescence intensity of CACDs at the optimal excitation wavelength was recorded every 10 min. As shown in Fig. 5d, the fluorescence intensity of the CACDs remained unchanged, indicating their good light resistance and stable fluorescence properties under UV irradiation. In order to better understand the long-term availability of CDs, the stability of the fluorescence intensity of CACDs hydrogels was observed over time at different pH, temperature, NaCl salt solution and UV irradiation over a period of 5 days. Fig. S6 shows that the CACDs hydrogels exhibit good stability.

The fluorescence spectra of CACDs treated with different metal ions under the same conditions were recorded. At the same doping concentration (0.1 M), the fluorescence of CACDs doped with Fe(II) dramatically decreased, whereas that of the CACDs doped with other common metal ions hardly changed (Fig. 7). The introduction of Fe(II) considerably reduced the fluorescence intensity of CACDs, primarily because of the presence of numerous -NH2 and -OH on the surface of CACDs, the strong coordination ability of Fe(II) as a coordination centre with the N atoms on the surface of CACDs and the chelating effect of Fe(II) with the N and O atoms in CACDs. The electrons of the coordinated atoms on the surface of CACDs enter the empty orbitals of the metal ions, which affect the conjugated surface structure of the CACDs, and these interactions bring the CDs in the hydrogel near each other, leading to fluorescence quenching [71,72] (Fig. 9).

Various metal ions reacted with CDs and CACDs to different degrees (Fig. 6a and b). While reacting with CDs, multivalent metal ions can crosslink CMC by bonding with -COOH on different units of the CMC chain, which affects the fluorescence intensity of CACDs. In addition, the fluorescence intensity relatively decreased by doping CACDs with Fe(III). However, the degree of fluorescence quenching of Fe(III) was considerably lower than that of Fe(II), suggesting that CACDs have a good selectivity for Fe(II) over Fe(III). This is in good agreement with the fluorescence quenching effect of metal ions on CDs, indicating the essential role of CDs in the fluorescence quenching of CACDs. As shown in Fig. S7a, CA did not show fluorescence before and after immersion in Fe(II), further suggesting that the CDs addition provide them with fluorescence properties. However, the addition of Fe(II) considerably enhanced the fluorescence quenching of CACDs compared to CDs, suggesting that CDs increase the crosslinking points of the polymer using CA as a carrier, allowing more functional groups to chelate and take up Fe(II). The numerous functional groups on the surface of the CACDs can act as ligands for chelating Fe(II), giving Fe(II) a strong affinity for the functional groups of CACDs. In addition, the overlap of the absorption spectra of CACDs with the fluorescence emission spectra or excitation spectra of CDs causes an internal filtration effect [73] (Fig. 6c), which affects the degree of fluorescence quenching. Moreover, because CACDs are solid-state materials, they are stable, portable and reusable, making them a suitable choice for rapid on-site detection of Fe(II).

Thus, to further evaluate and investigate whether Fe(II) ions can effectively quench the fluorescence of CACDs, their fluorescence spectra were obtained at an excitation wavelength of 420 nm by adding Fe(II) to a fluorescent hydrogel containing other metal ions (0.1 M) (Fig. 6d). The relative fluorescence intensity of CACDs was greatly quenched, indicating that the presence of other metals did not reduce the Fe(II) detection ability of CACDs. CACDs also exhibited high specificity and excellent Fe(II) detection performance.

To further investigate the ability of Fe(II) to effectively quench the fluorescence of CACDs, the sensitivity, linear response range and detection limit of CACDs were measured under optimal experimental conditions. As shown in Fig. 8a, the fluorescence intensity of the prepared CACDs was effectively quenched by Fe(II). Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of CACDs was sensitive to Fe(II) concentration and proportionally decreased with the increase of Fe(II) concentration. The pristine CACDs exhibited strong fluorescence intensity, which decreased by approximately 60 % with the addition of a Fe(II) solution with a concentration of only 100 μM. The fluorescence of the hydrogel can be almost completely quenched with the increase in the Fe(II) concentration to 1000 μM. As shown in Fig. S7b, increasing the concentration of Fe(II) from 0 to 1000 μM caused a change in the CACDs colour from slightly yellow to orange and a change in the fluorescence colour from bright fluorescence to a dark one under a UV light (365 nm). After 5 h, CACDs exhibited no apparent colour change under natural and UV (365 nm) lights. Therefore, the fluorescence colour change of CACDs can be directly used for the visual detection of Fe(II) by the naked eye. CACDs exhibited good linearity (y = 0.00654× + 1.01982) with an R2 value of 0.99587 at a Fe(II) concentration of 0–1000 μM (Fig. 8b). The minimum limit of detection was calculated according to the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency to be 0.22 μM. Thus, CACDs exhibited high detection sensitivity for low concentrations of Fe(II) solutions.

Unlike the numerous studies reported on CDs or CDs hydrogels for Fe(III) detection (Table 1 ), there are few studies on the detection of Fe(II). Among these few studies, those that reported better selectivity for Fe(II) detection than Fe(III) detection are rare. The present study is one of the few studies to realise Fe(II) detection with good sensitivity, high selectivity and convenient portability within a wide detection range. In addition, almost all CDs or CDs hydrogels are used for blue-fluorescence Fe(II) detection and/or Fe(III) detection, limiting their application in living organisms.

To confirm that the prepared CACDs could realise the detection of Fe(II) in real water samples, we collected real water samples, i.e. local lake water (Nanhu Park, Changchun), bottled mineral water (Quanyang Spring) and laboratory tap water, for testing (Table 2 ). The average recoveries of Fe(II) in the lake water, bottled mineral water and laboratory tap water were 98.76 %–103.50 %, 98.10 %–101.30 % and 100.36 %–101.90 %, respectively, and the relative standard deviations of Fe(II) detection (n = 3) was <0.8 %, indicating the high accuracy and reproducibility of CACDs for the detection of Fe(II) in real water samples [73]. These results suggest that CACDs can be a good candidate for Fe(II) detection.

The effect of CMC content as well as the change in crosslinker composition on adsorption was tested and is shown in Fig. S8.The CMC content of 2.0 wt% selected for this experiment as well as the crosslinker MBA presented the maximum adsorption capacity in the comparison. The CMC content had a subtle effect on the metal ion adsorption content of the CACDs hydrogels, but the effect on the metal ion adsorption content of the CMC content of 1.0 wt% showed a significant difference from the CMC content selected for this experiment, indicating that the CMC content has an effect on the adsorption capacity of the hydrogels.

The effect of adsorption of Fe(II) on CACDs hydrogels at different pH values was investigated. As shown in Fig. S9a, the adsorption amounts increases with increasing pH. This is mainly due to the decrease in protonation induced by the increase in pH and thus the enhancement of electrostatic attraction between CACDs hydrogels and Fe(II) [84]. Secondly, the decrease in H+ ion concentration mitigated the negative effect on competitive adsorption, which in turn increased Qe. pH = 7 was chosen for the adsorption experiments to focus on adsorption rather than chemical precipitation. The effect of CACDs hydrogels on Fe(II) adsorption was investigated at different temperatures. As shown in Fig. S9b, Qe appeared to increase with increasing temperature, indicating that the heat absorption reaction promotes the adsorption of Fe(II) on CACDs hydrogels [85]. In connection with the practical situation, the adsorption experiments were carried out at 25 °C.

The adsorption capacity of the hydrogel for Fe(II) was explored, and the adsorption capacity of CACDs was significantly increased to 202.28 mg/g compared with that of CA, indicating that the doping of CDs enhances the adsorption capacity of the hydrogel. Doping of CDs permits more functional groups to chelate and take up Fe(II), which results in a stronger affinity of CACDs for Fe(II). Thus CACDs are expected to be bifunctional materials for Fe(II) detection and removal.

Fig. 10a shows the adsorption of Fe(II) by the hydrogel over time. The actual adsorption of Fe(II) by the hydrogel reached equilibrium roughly within 210 min. The amount of adsorption increases with increasing contact time, and the CACDs adsorb more than the CA at any moment. The adsorption kinetic module of the hydrogel was investigated, and the results are shown in Fig. 10b and c. Comparing and analyzing the correlation coefficients of the pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order adsorption models, the correlation coefficients calculated by the pseudo-second-order kinetic model were closer to the unit, which led to the inference that the control mechanism of adsorption of Fe(II) by CACDs belongs to chemisorption.

The relationship between the adsorption amount of hydrogels and Fe(II) concentration was investigated. As shown in Fig. 10d, the adsorption amounts of hydrogels all increased with the increase of initial concentration. At the same initial concentration, the adsorption of CACDs was higher than that of CA. The adsorption isotherms of CACDs were investigated using Langmuir and Freundlich models (Fig. 10e-f). Among them, the Langmuir isotherm model was considered for single molecule adsorption. From the fitting results, the Langmuir adsorption isotherm model was more acceptable for the adsorption isotherm of Fe(II), which indicated that the adsorption of Fe(II) by CACDs was limited by the monolayer coverage. In summary, the adsorption process of Fe(II) on CACDs involves not only the chelation of functional groups with Fe(II), but also the encapsulation of Fe(II) in the hydrogel.

Herein, a fluorescent hydrogel (CACDs) with strong and bright yellow fluorescence was prepared by doping CDs into crosslinked AM-grafted CMC, which was synthesised via a simple, rapid, inexpensive, safe and non-polluting method. CACDs achieved direct, effective and specific detection of Fe(II) at an Fe(II) concentration of 0–1000 μM with no interference owing to the internal filtration effect, which increases fluorescence quenching. This provides a strong foundation for the detection of Fe(II) in cells and other biological systems using CACDs. The lowest detection limit of Fe(II) was 0.22 μM, which was considerably lower than the maximum level of Fe in drinking water in China (5.35 μM). The prepared CACDs exhibited good, accurate, reproducible Fe(II) detection in real water samples. Therefore, CACDs can be used in practice as an effective portable platform for Fe(II) detection.
